seoTitle: Slow WordPress on an editorial product: module-by-module optimization | Luc Rousseau
seoDescription: Slow WordPress, comparator or high traffic: stabilization, deep perf, module-by-module rebuild (blocks, APIs). Editorial product, not agency maintenance. Quebec.

hero.quote: Our WordPress holds, but every comparator or editorial launch costs too much in perf and debt.
hero.title: Slow WordPress on an editorial product?
hero.lede:
You may be searching for « WordPress optimization » or « WordPress performance consultant ». On an editorial product (comparator, multi-brand, conversion), the real issue isn't another plugin layer: it's a product platform that must hold under traffic and evolve every week.

I work in a fractional product-tech mandate: stabilize prod, optimize module by module (Gutenberg, headless, APIs, comparators), evaluate continuously whether the stack should stay or migrate. Fractional product-tech mandate, not brochure maintenance.

For full support (often WordPress, sometimes another stack): Editorial product at scale. Archaic stack to migrate: Archaic WordPress: headless or Laravel by phases.

blocks.title: Symptoms vs product approach
blocks.intro: Slow WordPress on a brochure site and slow WordPress on a comparator at millions of visits aren't the same severity or the same levers.

panels.heading: What you see
items: Load times hurting conversion and SEO on high-traffic journeys
Piled-on plugins, legacy theme, debt slowing every editorial feature
Team in firefighting mode: perf and stability before every comparator or campaign launch

panels.heading: What I address
items: Product-oriented audit: conversion journeys, blocks, APIs, not PageSpeed alone
Module-by-module rebuild: comparators, Gutenberg patterns, headless layer when it pays off
Recurring fractional mandate: stabilize, ship, re-evaluate (migration only if the stack truly blocks you)

blocks.title: Already delivered at this scale
blocks.lede: Concrete references: Milesopedia (multi-brand comparators, high traffic) and the Nesto ecosystem (Gutenberg blocks, APIs, leads into CRM). Same product logic, whether the stack stays coupled WordPress, headless, or hybrid.

blocks.title: Stabilize, optimize, evolve
blocks.lede: Three typical levers on WordPress carrying an editorial product, without a « magic patch » promise.

items.title: Stabilization and release frame
items.content: Incidents, debt, deploys: reduce firefighting before accelerating. Clear criteria for « prod that holds » for editorial and marketing teams.

items.title: Deep performance
items.content: Cache, CDN, queries, heavy blocks, comparators: optimization by module, not plugin accumulation. Headless or APIs when the front must decouple.

items.title: Continuous product evolution
items.content: New journeys, partnerships, monetization: reusable blocks and API contracts so editorial ships without a one-off every launch.

blocks.title: Technologies involved
blocks.lede: Often WordPress (Gutenberg, headless, WPGraphQL/REST); sometimes Laravel, React/Next.js, or hybrid stack depending on the product.
items: Perf audit · plugins · debt · conversion journeys
Redis · cache · CDN · queries under traffic

items.title: Is this WordPress maintenance?
items.content: Fractional product-tech mandate on a living editorial platform: comparators, conversion, APIs, perf module by module. Scope covers architecture, evolution, and product delivery, not plugin maintenance.

items.title: Plugin optimization or module-by-module rebuild?
items.content: Both can coexist in a phase, but the target is a system that absorbs launches: blocks, patterns, APIs. Plugins alone don't hold on a high-traffic comparator.

items.title: Do we need headless or a Laravel migration?
items.content: Not by default. We optimize and evolve while the product holds. If debt or architecture truly block you: phased rebuild (headless or Laravel), no big bang.

items.title: Where is the full offer (not WordPress-only)?
items.content: Editorial product at scale: fractional mandate on content + conversion + monetization platform, editorial platforms at scale, often WordPress.

items.title: Why isn't this page in the Situations menu?
items.content: It targets specific searches (slow WordPress, editorial perf) and points to the fractional product-tech mandate. Situations describe how I work; this page helps if you landed from Google.

blocks.label: Discuss your platform
blocks.teaser: Slow WordPress on a comparator or editorial product? One call to see if stabilization and module-by-module optimization are enough, or if a phased rebuild is worth it.
